findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete jscpd invocation + output-parsing specification to the plan (e.g., `jscpd --reporters json` and the JSON field path for the duplicate percentage/count), or reference an existing wrapper — the plan currently asserts jscpd is usable with no grounding in the repo.
evidence: Plan states: 'run scc/lizard (complexity) and jscpd (clones) over the tree and append snapshots via snapshot.py'
search_files for 'jscpd' across src/rebar returns zero matches
scc/lizard ARE grounded: src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py contains parsing logic for them
No jscpd output format parser, invocation wrapper, or configuration exists in the repo to rely on
findings.finding: `jscpd` is asserted as a runnable, parseable clone-detection tool, but it has zero presence in the repository — no wrapper, no output parser, no config, and no prior reference — making the claim that the backfill script can invoke and parse `jscpd` output an unbacked assertion.
findings.location: Approach section: 'run `scc`/`lizard` (complexity) and `jscpd` (clones) over the tree'; Files section: `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`
scenarios: The new script must invent jscpd invocation and output parsing from scratch with no existing infrastructure to reference or reuse, yet the plan presents this as routine — if jscpd's JSON/XML output schema isn't specified in the plan, implementers may produce an incompatible `clone_count` key.
AC requires `clone_count` key in each snapshot; without a specified jscpd output parsing contract, the test fixture stub and the real invocation may diverge silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's Approach section: 'Invoke `jscpd --reporters json <path>` and extract `statistics.clones.percentage` (or the relevant field) as `clone_count`; document the exact field path so the stub in the test mirrors real output structure.' Also note that unlike scc/lizard (which have parsing infrastructure in engine_b_backends.py), jscpd parsing must be written from scratch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Approach section that `ts` passed to `write_snapshot` for each sampled commit must be derived from that commit's date (e.g., `git log -1 --format=%cI <sha>`), not the wall-clock time of the backfill run.
evidence: write_snapshot signature: `write_snapshot(record: dict, *, repo_root: str | os.PathLike[str], ts: str) -> None` — ts is caller-supplied
Plan says 'run scc/lizard (complexity) and jscpd (clones) over the tree and append snapshots via snapshot.py' but never states which date feeds `ts`
Plan says 'Writes through the same snapshot.py writer as the going-forward path' — the going-forward path uses current time, but backfill MUST use commit date to produce a valid historical series
findings.finding: The plan never specifies what ISO-8601 timestamp value is passed as `ts` to `write_snapshot` for each backfilled commit. Using wall-clock time (the time the backfill runs) instead of the commit's author/committer date would silently store all backfilled snapshots with today's timestamp, corrupting the historical time series the feature is meant to produce.
findings.location: Approach section / Files section: `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`
scenarios: Implementor defaults to datetime.utcnow() for `ts`, all 3 backfilled snapshots get today's timestamp — trends appear flat/instantaneous rather than historical
Test with mocked tool output passes regardless of ts value, so the gap survives testing undetected
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'For each sampled commit, `ts` is set to that commit's ISO-8601 committer date (obtained via `git log -1 --format=%cI <sha>`), so backfilled snapshots land at their true historical positions in the time series.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Realign the leaf's backfill label to `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified` (the parent's mandated single backfill vocabulary) and add an AC asserting those exact labels are present on every written snapshot.
evidence: Parent: 'every backfilled/classified value is labeled `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified` (the single backfill vocabulary — there is no `backfill_structural`)'
Parent AC5: 'at least one backfilled `caused_by`/`close_class`/snapshot value exists carrying `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified`'
Leaf Approach: 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`' — both the source tag and confidence level diverge from the parent's mandated vocabulary
The parent explicitly calls this 'the single backfill vocabulary', leaving no room for the leaf to introduce a different tag
findings.finding: The leaf labels backfilled snapshots `source=snapshot, confidence=high`, directly contradicting the parent's explicit single backfill vocabulary (`source=backfill_classified`, `confidence=classified`) and the parent's AC5, which asserts that snapshot backfill values must carry exactly `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified`.
findings.location: Leaf §Approach: 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`'; Parent §Success Criteria (backfill vocabulary bullet) and AC5
scenarios: The metrics command's `is_authoritative()` check reads `source` to exclude backfilled values from authoritative aggregation; a `source=snapshot` label would be treated as authoritative (or unknown), silently contaminating structural series
Parent AC5 integration test asserts `source=backfill_classified` on snapshot values — the leaf's output would fail that assertion
findings.suggested_fix: In §Approach, replace 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`' with 'snapshots are labeled `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified`'. Add to AC1: verify each snapshot carries `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified` (e.g., `jq -e '.source == "backfill_classified" and .confidence == "classified"'`). Add an AC asserting `is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` returns False for these snapshots.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify which tool (`scc` vs `lizard`) provides the `complexity` value, name the exact CLI flags and JSON output field used (e.g. `lizard --output-file - -f json` producing `average_cyclomatic_complexity`), and confirm this is distinct from the LOC metric scc provides.
evidence: src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py line 376: `harness.run_tool([binary, '--format', 'json', str(repo_root)], ...)` with `binary` resolved from `_METRIC_CANDIDATES = ('scc', 'lizard')`
engine_b_backends.py line 413: `entry.get('Code', 0)` — scc JSON output key is 'Code' (lines), not complexity

Evidence class: Contradicted — scc's known JSON schema does not include a cyclomatic-complexity field; lizard does but its CLI output format/flags for machine-readable complexity are unspecified in the plan
findings.finding: The plan asserts `scc` produces a `complexity` metric, but the existing codebase integration shows scc's `--format json` output contains per-file `Code` (lines of code) counts — not cyclomatic complexity. Cyclomatic complexity is lizard's domain. Using scc as the complexity source is a capability gap: the plan's `complexity` key cannot be derived from scc's output without additional logic or a switch to lizard's output format.
findings.location: Files section / Approach section: 'run scc/lizard (complexity)'
scenarios: Script invokes scc for complexity, parses its JSON, finds no complexity field, writes 0 or crashes — backfill produces silent garbage data
Or script invokes lizard for complexity but no flag/output-format specification means the implementer must guess the correct invocation
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'scc/lizard (complexity)' with two separate tool invocations: `scc --format json <path>` for LOC-based size, and `lizard -o json <path>` (verify flag via `lizard --help`) for cyclomatic complexity. Define which field from each output maps to the `complexity` snapshot key.

findings.finding: `jscpd` is invoked in the repo exclusively via `npx jscpd` (requiring Node.js/npm), but the plan names it as a bare `jscpd` CLI with no acknowledgment of the npx/Node.js runtime dependency. Additionally, jscpd's `--reporters json` output is a structured object (a `duplicates` array with clone instances), not a bare integer — the plan asserts a parseable `clone_count` key but provides no specification of how that count is derived from jscpd's actual JSON schema.
findings.location: Files section: 'jscpd (clones)'; Approach section: 'run jscpd (clones)'
scenarios: Script calls `jscpd` as a bare binary; it is not on PATH (only `npx jscpd` works); the tool run fails and every commit is skipped
Script calls `npx jscpd` but parses output for a `clone_count` key that does not exist in jscpd's actual JSON structure (which uses a `duplicates` array)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files/Approach section, specify: invocation is `npx jscpd <path> --reporters json --output <tmpdir>`, and `clone_count` is derived as `len(output['statistics']['clones'])` or equivalent field (verify against `jscpd --help` and a sample run). Add Node.js as an explicit runtime precondition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the re-run policy in the plan (e.g. 'skip a commit if a snapshot with matching commit SHA already exists in the store') so the executor has a concrete design decision to implement.
evidence: Plan describes writing snapshots via snapshot.py but says nothing about deduplication or re-run behavior
'One-time backfill' label does not prevent accidental re-execution
findings.finding: The plan is silent on idempotency: if the backfill script is run twice against the same store, it will write duplicate snapshots for the same commits, potentially corrupting the trend series. An executor must invent a design choice (skip-if-exists, overwrite, or error) with no guidance.
findings.location: Approach section / Files section: `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`
scenarios: Operator runs the script, realizes the date range was wrong, adjusts, and re-runs — duplicate snapshots now appear in the store for already-backfilled commits
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'Before writing, check whether a snapshot tagged with the commit SHA already exists in the store; if so, skip and log — making the script idempotent on re-run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the tree-isolation mechanism for historical checkouts (e.g., `git worktree add --detach`, temp `git clone --local`, or `repo_snapshot.py`'s `read-tree`+`checkout-index` approach) so the implementer doesn't re-derive it or mutate the live working tree.
evidence: Plan: 'by checking out sampled historical commits, running the metric tools'
src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py: 'git read-tree <sha> into a *temp* index (GIT_INDEX_FILE) → git checkout-index --all --prefix=<tmp>/' — production lock-free tree materialization already exists
repo_snapshot.py docstring: 'neither read-tree nor checkout-index touches the repo's own index.lock/config.lock or working tree, so two materializations of different SHAs never contend'

The backfill script needs to run tools against historical trees without corrupting the live working tree — this is exactly the problem repo_snapshot.py solves, though its primary consumer is the gate system
findings.finding: The plan says 'checking out sampled historical commits' but does not specify the isolation mechanism. A naive `git checkout` mutates the live working tree; the codebase already has `src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py` which provides lock-free, crash-safe tree materialization (`read-tree` + `checkout-index`) for exactly this scenario. The plan neither reuses that infrastructure nor explains why a simpler mechanism (temp clone, `git worktree add --detach`) is sufficient, leaving the implementer to independently re-derive a tree-isolation approach.
findings.location: Approach section — 'checking out sampled historical commits'
scenarios: Implementer writes `git checkout <sha>` in the live working tree, corrupting the current state and breaking concurrent operations.
Implementer independently builds a temp-clone approach, duplicating reasoning already encoded in repo_snapshot.py's design rationale (ADR 0005).
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach: 'Each sampled commit is materialized into a throwaway temp directory via `git clone --local --no-hardlinks <repo> <tmpdir> && git -C <tmpdir> checkout <sha>` (or `git worktree add --detach`), so the live working tree is never touched.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `git checkout` is preferred over the existing `_materialize_tree()` / `git read-tree + checkout-index` primitive in `src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py`, or adopt that primitive to avoid working-tree mutation and index lock contention.
evidence: Plan phrase: 'which git makes deterministic (checkout + tool run at each sampled commit)'

src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py: `_materialize_tree()` uses `git read-tree <sha>` + `git checkout-index --all --prefix=<tmp>/` with `GIT_INDEX_FILE` env override — explicitly designed to 'never touch the repo's own index/working tree'
Existing mechanism is already used by the grounding/gating subsystem for exactly this use case (materializing a committed tree at a given SHA for tool runs)
ABSENCE: plan does not mention repo_snapshot or read-tree/checkout-index as an option
findings.finding: The plan proposes 'checking out sampled historical commits' (implying `git checkout`) to materialize trees for tool runs, but the repo already has a lock-free, worktree-safe materialization primitive — `_materialize_tree()` in `src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py` — that uses `git read-tree + checkout-index` with a throwaway GIT_INDEX_FILE to avoid touching the working tree. Choosing a naive `git checkout` approach over this existing prior art is unjustified and risks working-tree corruption or index lock contention.
findings.location: Approach section: 'git makes deterministic (checkout + tool run at each sampled commit)'
scenarios: Operator runs backfill with uncommitted changes in the working tree — `git checkout` discards or conflicts with them
Backfill is run concurrently with another process that holds index.lock — naive checkout deadlocks or errors
Partial checkout failure at mid-history commit leaves working tree at an unexpected commit
findings.suggested_fix: Use `repo_snapshot._materialize_tree(repo_root, sha, dest_tmp)` (or its public wrapper) for each sampled commit: it materializes the full tree into a temp directory without touching the live working tree or index, making the backfill safe to run at any time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit cardinality assertion to AC1, e.g. `assert len(snapshots) == 3` in the test or `jq -e 'length == 3'` against the written snapshot collection.
evidence: AC1 states '3 snapshots' but the jq -e check is described only for key presence, not count
Defect taxonomy (5): 'missing cardinality assertion when the criterion names a count'
findings.finding: AC1 names a count ('3 snapshots') but the proving command (`jq -e`) has no cardinality assertion, so a test that writes fewer snapshots but includes the right keys could satisfy the criterion — per defect-taxonomy item (5).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: 'sampling 3 fixture commits writes 3 snapshots each containing complexity and clone_count keys (jq -e)'
scenarios: A bug causes one sampled commit to silently produce no snapshot; the test still passes because the two snapshots that were written have the right keys
findings.suggested_fix: In the test, after calling the backfill function with 3 fixture commits, assert `assert len(written_snapshots) == 3` before asserting key presence; or in the jq command use `jq -e '[.[] | select(has("complexity") and has("clone_count"))] | length == 3'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the working-tree pre-condition and restore strategy in the plan (e.g. 'requires a clean working tree; script restores HEAD via `git checkout -` in a finally block, or operates on a bare/worktree clone to avoid touching the developer's working tree').
evidence: No mention of `git stash`, `git checkout -` restore, or a requirement for a clean working tree
ABSENCE: no constraint on pre-conditions for running the script
findings.finding: The plan does not specify git working-tree safety: the script checks out historical commits, but it is silent on what happens if the working tree is dirty (uncommitted changes) or how HEAD is restored after the backfill completes. An executor must invent this behavior.
findings.location: Approach section: 'git makes deterministic (checkout + tool run at each sampled commit)'
scenarios: Developer runs the script mid-feature with staged changes; the checkout silently discards or conflicts with those changes, or HEAD is left at an old commit after an error
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'The script asserts a clean working tree at startup (or uses `git worktree add` / a temp bare clone) and restores the original HEAD in a finally block, logging any failure to restore.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the script records the current HEAD ref before starting, restores it on both normal exit and exception/interrupt (e.g., `try/finally`), and that the AC includes a test asserting HEAD is restored after the backfill run.
evidence: 'checking out sampled historical commits' — implies `git checkout <sha>` for each sampled commit
No mention of `git checkout -` or restoring HEAD/branch after the loop
No mention of what happens if the script is interrupted mid-checkout (dirty detached HEAD, lost branch pointer)
T9: the git working tree is shared mutable state used by the developer and potentially other tools simultaneously
findings.finding: The script performs `git checkout` of historical commits to gather metrics, mutating the working tree (shared mutable state), but the plan specifies no restoration of HEAD after the backfill completes and no handling of the abandon path (e.g., Ctrl-C mid-checkout), leaving the repository in a detached-HEAD state.
findings.location: Approach section: 'checking out sampled historical commits, running the metric tools'
scenarios: A developer runs the script on their workstation, interrupts it with Ctrl-C at commit 2 of 20 — their working tree is left at a historical commit in detached-HEAD state, and any editor or build tool operating concurrently sees the wrong source tree.
The backfill completes successfully but HEAD is left pointing at the last sampled historical commit rather than the branch tip, surprising any subsequent git operations.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the checkout loop in a `try/finally` block: capture `git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD` (or the SHA for detached HEAD) before starting, and run `git checkout <original-ref>` in the `finally` clause. Add an AC verifying HEAD is restored.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an idempotency guard keyed on (commit_sha, metric_type) before writing each snapshot, mirroring the `_existing_ticket_ids()` pattern in `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py`, so re-running the backfill is safe.
evidence: snapshot.py write_snapshot(): plain open('a') append with no deduplication logic
scripts/backfill_bug_class.py _existing_ticket_ids(): idempotency guard pattern already established in the codebase
ABSENCE: the plan does not mention any deduplication or idempotency check before writing
findings.finding: The plan appends snapshots via `write_snapshot()` with no idempotency guard. `write_snapshot()` in `snapshot.py` is a plain append — it has no deduplication. Re-running the backfill script (on failure/retry, operator mistake, or CI re-trigger) will silently append duplicate snapshot entries for the same historical commits, corrupting the time-series store. The analogous backfill script in this repo (`scripts/backfill_bug_class.py`) explicitly solves this with `_existing_ticket_ids()`. No equivalent guard is specified for (commit_sha, metric_type) in this plan.
findings.location: Approach section: 'append snapshots via src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py'
scenarios: Script is interrupted after writing 2 of 3 snapshots and re-run: first 2 commits get duplicate entries
Operator runs twice by mistake: every historical snapshot is doubled, skewing trend charts
findings.suggested_fix: Before appending, read existing snapshots from the store and collect the set of already-written commit SHAs; skip any commit whose snapshot is already present. The snapshot record should include the source commit SHA as a field to enable this check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an idempotency guard so re-running the backfill script does not produce duplicate snapshots (e.g., check for an existing snapshot keyed on commit SHA before writing, or implement upsert semantics in the write path).
evidence: Plan says 'append snapshots via src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py' with no mention of deduplication, upsert, or existence check before writing.
AC does not test or assert idempotent behaviour (two runs of the backfill produce the same store state).
findings.finding: The backfill script appends snapshots with no idempotency guard: if the script is run more than once (re-run on failure, operator mistake, CI retry), it will write duplicate snapshot entries for the same commits, silently corrupting the historical series.
findings.location: Approach / 'append snapshots via snapshot.py'
scenarios: Operator re-runs the script after a partial failure.
CI retries the backfill job.
A second monthly cadence run overlaps with the first.
findings.suggested_fix: Before calling snapshot.py for each commit, query whether a snapshot with that commit SHA already exists; if so, skip the write and log a 'already backfilled' message. Add an AC assertion: running the script twice on the same fixture commits leaves exactly N snapshots (not 2N).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an idempotency guard: before writing a snapshot for a sampled commit, check whether a snapshot for that commit SHA already exists in the store and skip if so (document this in the AC).
evidence: 'append snapshots via `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py`' — 'append' implies no duplicate detection
No mention of a guard such as 'skip if snapshot for this commit already exists' or a deduplication step
Shared store is mutated by both the backfill script and the going-forward path; a second backfill run would double-count history
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'append snapshots' with no idempotency guard. Re-running the backfill script a second time will silently append duplicate snapshots for the same commits to the shared store, corrupting trend data.
findings.location: Approach section: 'append snapshots via `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py`'
scenarios: A developer runs the script, realises they used the wrong cadence, adjusts the arg, and re-runs — the store now contains two snapshot series for overlapping commits, skewing all complexity/clone trend charts.
findings.suggested_fix: In `snapshot.py` or in the backfill script, query existing snapshots by commit SHA before appending; skip and log 'already backfilled' if a record is found. Add an AC: 're-running the script on already-backfilled commits writes no new records.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add idempotency to the backfill script: before writing a snapshot for a sampled commit SHA, check whether a record with that commit SHA (or equivalent dedup key) already exists in the NDJSON store and skip it — matching the pattern in `backfill_bug_class.py`'s `_existing_ticket_ids()`.
evidence: snapshot.py write_snapshot() opens the file with mode 'a' and unconditionally appends — no dedup logic anywhere in the implementation
The sibling script backfill_bug_class.py explicitly implements `_existing_ticket_ids()` to guard against double-writes; this plan has no equivalent
Plan states 'append snapshots via snapshot.py' with no mention of idempotency or skip-if-already-present logic
AC says '3 fixture commits writes 3 snapshots' but does not assert exactly-once semantics on re-run
findings.finding: The backfill script writes through `write_snapshot()`, which is a pure append with no dedup guard on commit SHA. If the script is interrupted and re-run, every already-processed commit gets a second (or third) snapshot appended, silently inflating the trend series without any error.
findings.location: Approach section / `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py` (new file)
scenarios: Backfill is killed after 20 of 60 monthly commits are processed; operator re-runs the script — commits 1-20 are written twice, skewing every trend-line average and count-based assertion downstream.
CI runs the backfill as a one-time job and the job is retried on transient failure — same duplication outcome.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_already_backfilled_commits(artifact: Path) -> set[str]` helper that reads existing records and collects their commit SHA field, then skip any commit whose SHA is already present before calling `write_snapshot()`. Include a test assertion that running the script twice against the same fixture repo still produces exactly 3 snapshots (not 6).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC 1) to assert that each written snapshot contains `source=snapshot` and `confidence=high` fields, matching the label described in the Approach section.
evidence: 'snapshots are labeled source=snapshot, confidence=high (deterministic tool output)'
No AC references source or confidence keys in the snapshot output.
findings.finding: The Approach section describes snapshots labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high` as a concrete output property, but no AC verifies this label is present in the written snapshots.
findings.location: Approach section; Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: An implementer omits the labels entirely; no test catches the regression.
A future consumer relying on source=snapshot filtering finds unlabeled backfilled snapshots.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the sampling-cadence script argument exists and that the default value produces monthly-spaced commit selection (e.g., passing `--cadence monthly` or omitting the arg selects one commit per month from the fixture repo).
evidence: 'Sampling cadence is a script arg (default monthly) — a stated default.'
Neither AC 1 nor AC 2 mentions the CLI argument or its default behavior.
findings.finding: The Approach section describes the sampling cadence as 'a script arg (default monthly)' — an explicit CLI interface — but no AC verifies the argument exists or that the default produces monthly sampling.
findings.location: Approach section; Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: The implementer hard-codes monthly sampling instead of wiring a CLI argument; no test catches this.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Tests section label: replace 'Unit' with 'Scripts-tier' (or equivalent) so both sections consistently identify this as the scripts tier, matching `pytestmark = pytest.mark.scripts`.
evidence: Tests section: 'Unit (temp git repo + stubbed tool output)'
Test tier section: 'The test lives under tests/scripts/ with pytestmark = pytest.mark.scripts (the scripts tier)'
findings.finding: The Tests section labels the test 'Unit (temp git repo + stubbed tool output)' while the Test tier section names the same test 'scripts tier' — two different tier names for the same test could send an implementer in contradictory directions about how to categorise, mark, and run it.
findings.location: Tests section ('Unit (temp git repo…)'); Test tier section ('scripts tier; pytestmark = pytest.mark.scripts')
scenarios: An implementer reads 'Unit' and applies pytest.mark.unit instead of pytest.mark.scripts, causing the test to be excluded from scripts-tier CI runs.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Tests section header to: 'Scripts-tier (temp git repo + stubbed tool output):'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one fixture containing real sample output from each tool (scc, lizard, jscpd) captured from a known invocation, and assert the parser produces the expected metric values against that fixture — without requiring the tools to be installed in CI.
evidence: 'External tools are stubbed/mocked so the test is deterministic and needs no scc/lizard/jscpd installed' — plan explicitly stubs all three tools.
No smoke test, integration fixture, or sample real-output snapshot is mentioned to validate the parsing logic against actual tool output.
findings.finding: All external tool integrations (scc/lizard/jscpd output parsing) are validated only against mocked/stubbed output. The actual output format of these tools — which the script must parse — is never exercised against a real invocation, so format mismatches would silently produce wrong metric values or unhandled parse errors at backfill time.
findings.location: Test tier / 'External tools are stubbed/mocked so the test is deterministic'
scenarios: scc JSON schema changes between versions
lizard output format differs from what the parser expects
jscpd output format is environment-dependent
findings.suggested_fix: Create `tests/scripts/fixtures/scc_output.json`, `lizard_output.txt`, and `jscpd_output.json` with captured real outputs; parameterise a parser unit test against them so the parsing logic is validated against the actual tool format, not a hand-crafted mock.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the deployment-shape prerequisite (script must be run from inside a local checkout of the target project) and, if remote-MCP support is desired, specify an alternative approach (e.g., `git worktree add` in a temp dir or `git archive | tar` to a temp workspace).
evidence: 'checking out sampled historical commits, running the metric tools, and writing snapshots' — requires `git checkout` against a local working tree
Supported matrix cell: 'server outside the checkout' — rebar deployed as a remote MCP server with no unrestricted local filesystem access to the target project
The plan contains no fallback for remote or bare-repository access (e.g., `git archive`, worktrees in a temp dir)
findings.finding: The script assumes a locally accessible, writable git working tree for `git checkout` operations. In the 'server outside the checkout' deployment shape (rebar running as a remote MCP server not co-located with the target project), no local working tree is available, so the script cannot check out historical commits.
findings.location: Approach section: 'checking out sampled historical commits, running the metric tools'
scenarios: A team running rebar as a remote MCP server (no local checkout of the target project) attempts to run the backfill script — `git checkout` fails immediately with 'not a git repository' or 'permission denied', and no snapshots are written, with no actionable error message explaining the deployment-shape limitation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `git checkout <sha>` on the live working tree with a safe isolated-tree mechanism (e.g. `git read-tree + git checkout-index` into a temp dir per commit, mirroring `src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py`), and add a guard that refuses to run if the working tree is dirty.
evidence: Plan: 'by checking out sampled historical commits, running the metric tools'
ADR 0005 D1: 'Rejected alternative: git worktree add per ref — takes repo-level index/config locks (the exact contention we avoid)'
src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py already implements read-tree + checkout-index into a temp prefix, the codebase-aligned safer mechanism
ABSENCE: the plan specifies no working-tree restoration step, no dirty-working-tree guard, and no mechanism for safe concurrent use
findings.finding: The plan's core mechanism — 'checking out sampled historical commits' via `git checkout` over the live working tree — mutates the shared working tree, requires explicit post-commit state restoration (not specified), and is unsafe under any concurrent use. The repo has already documented this as the inferior/rejected approach (ADR 0005 D1: '`git worktree add` per ref — takes repo-level index/config locks ... Rejected') and already ships a safer mechanism (`src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py` uses `git read-tree + checkout-index` into a throwaway temp dir per SHA, which is lock-free and never touches the working tree). A backfill script that calls `git checkout <old-sha>` mid-run leaves the repo in detached HEAD state if the script crashes, and will corrupt any dirty working tree present at invocation time.
findings.location: Approach section: 'git makes deterministic (checkout + tool run at each sampled commit)'
scenarios: Script crashes between commits: repo left in detached HEAD on an old commit, breaking all subsequent git operations in the same process
Operator runs backfill while agent has uncommitted work: git checkout destroys or stashes those changes silently
CI runner or another tool reads the repo while backfill is mid-checkout: reads wrong-era file content
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git read-tree <sha>` into a per-commit `GIT_INDEX_FILE` and `git checkout-index --all --prefix=<tmpdir>/` to materialize each historical tree in an isolated temp directory; run scc/lizard/jscpd against that temp directory; clean it up after each commit. This is exactly the pattern in `repo_snapshot.py` and avoids all working-tree mutation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence explaining why the chosen tree-access mechanism was selected (or, if switching to the safer `read-tree + checkout-index` path, state that rationale instead).
evidence: Plan: only states 'git makes deterministic (checkout + tool run at each sampled commit)' — no rationale for working-tree checkout vs isolation
ADR 0005 D1: explicitly documents the safer alternative and why it was chosen over working-tree mutation
ABSENCE: no positive rationale for the checkout approach in the plan
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing direct `git checkout` (working-tree mutation) over the already-available `read-tree + checkout-index` isolation mechanism. Given that ADR 0005 in this same repo explicitly records `git worktree add` (similar in impact) as the REJECTED approach and documents the exact safer alternative, the absence of rationale for the chosen mechanism leaves the implementer without guidance on why the riskier path was chosen — and may lead them to discover the safer mechanism independently and deviate from the plan.
findings.location: Approach section
scenarios: Implementer reads ADR 0005 and questions the plan's mechanism, leading to untracked deviation

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the rollback path explicitly: assert that because `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is git-tracked, `git checkout HEAD -- .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` fully restores pre-backfill state, and add a note in the script's `--help` / docstring directing operators to use this command on failure.
evidence: Plan: 'Writes through the same snapshot.py writer' — no mention of rollback or undo
snapshot.py has no delete/truncate/rollback API; write_snapshot() opens with mode 'a'
Plan does not state that the file is git-tracked and therefore `git checkout .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is the rollback path
ABSENCE: no AC covers what the store looks like after a failed or aborted backfill run
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a back-out / rollback path for a partially-completed or incorrectly-executed backfill. Since `write_snapshot()` is append-only, bad rows cannot be removed without manual file surgery, and the plan does not assert the post-rollback state of the NDJSON file.
findings.location: Approach section / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: Backfill writes 30 snapshots with an incorrect tool-output parser (bug in jscpd parsing), then the operator discovers the error. There is no defined procedure to remove the bad rows — the file must be manually edited or the entire NDJSON file reverted via git.
Partial failure leaves half the history written; operator must know to `git checkout` the file to restore a clean state, but the plan never says this.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
